title: Teaching
text: My teaching is committedly participatory. My students are required to prepare before each session to be able to ask and respond to questions. I design my teaching to prompt knowledge and critical thinking; my interventions are, therefore, more questions than answers. I do not expect my students to know the answers but to be able to think with and against me. I believe this method helps instilling fundamental academic and work skills: tolerance to frustration and uncertainty, and responsibility. Two of my undergraduate professors influenced this style: Luis Rodríguez Collao and Enzo Solari Alliende. I owe them ability to think.

title: Teaching
text:
2025-2026 University of Cambridge
I currently supervise undergraduate students in the course of Statistics and Methods.
During Michaelmas I was the teaching assistant of Introduction to Quantitative Analysis. I was also an invited lecturer for Criminological Research Methods in the class of Critiquing Quantitative Research. Both for the Master’s in Criminology

title: Teaching
text:
2024-2025 University of Cambridge
I supervised undergraduate students in the course of Foundations in Criminology and Criminal Justice.
I was the teaching assistant of Introduction to Quantitative Analysis and an invited lecturer for Criminological Research Methods in the class of using LLMs, both for the Master’s in Criminology.

title: Natalia Cabrera-Morales
text:
Viento trágico de alas negras azota al guásimo y al yarumo. Gimiendo van por la quebrada Viento acre olor a humo.
Solloza angustia amarga el obscuro platanal y danza loca la llama en el rubio maizal.
Hay hombres persiguiendo, hombres que atacan y hombres huyendo.
Arde el ranchito blanco allá en la verde loma. Sombras sigilosas por el barranco
Después…. silencio… y cenizas. Y mil cuervos hambrientos en picada bajo la nube plomiza.
En el regazo de la tierra Cuajan bermejas flores Y en manojos apretados nieva La cal, sus faldas de colores.
Turbias las aguas del rio Aguas con sabor a sangre Envolviendo cuerpos fríos. Corazones en su canto silenciados, Que ayer fueon primavera; De anhelos y promesa iluminados, Como el cielo de la luna plena.
Pobre hombre amasado en tinieblas. ¿No sientes que tu alma la oprimen resinas muy negras?
En tus labuos se helará la sonrisa y en tus manos tejidas de besos ya no podrán anidar las caricias.
¡Oh hermano!… Tal vez, no tienes la culpa… Se olvidaron los siglos del pobre Caín, y Caín sigue errando por la tierra madura y empuña tu mano, por ver si él puede morir.
Colombia sangra en silencio, Ella misma clávase en el madero. Se ha olvidado de Jesús el Nazareno y de su voz clamorosa en el desierto.
Esta patria enlutada no es la mía, Pero es pétalo fulgente de América Latina.
Pero su dolor callado, y su profunda herida, Están filtrando por mi alma estremecida.
Elcira Caamaño Gutiérrez

title: Natalia Cabrera-Morales
text:
I have a small dog whose name is Pasqui. She is my best friend and constant companion. I grew up between two countryside towns close to the mountains in Chile Los Andes and Calle Larga, at 17 I moved to Valparaíso which I consider my hometown. My mom was a nutritionist in a rural health centre, so I grew up accompanying her at work and seeing her interact with her patients with care and interest. My mom was always the economic head of the household.



I also grew up hearing stories about conflict and political struggle, as my father was displaced from his home in Colombia as product of the conflict when he was 10. His mother used to write poems and paint. My favourite poem of hers is about her experience with the early days of the conflict. My father also had to leave Chile back to Colombia during his youth as a consequence of the dictatorship.





My research is influenced by my upbringing in many ways. But also by many other life experiences. During my undergrad I used to frequent the prison in Valparaíso to volunteer in a few projects, which for ever impacted my understanding of punishment and my perception of people in prison. Regarding the action of the police, I witnessed their behaviour frequently during protests in Chile. A few of my friends suffered torture and other forms of police violence.



However, during my master’s in Sociology I had the opportunity to interview several police officers. One of them was imprisoned being charged with torture. It struck me how sweet and conscientious he was. He had a calm and cheerful demeanour and cared about vulnerable people. The most prominent value underlying his discourse was service to society. All of these experiences have taught me the importance to honour the complexity of the world, avoid simplification and empathise with those who are frequently despised.





My volunteering and activism have included, several projects in the prison in Valparaíso, including coordinating the participation of prisoners in the constitutional discussion in 2017. In 2019 drafted a bill project to ban rubber bullets that was presented in the congress by the MP Jorge Brito Hasbún. Also in 2019, I presented a protection appeal to ban rubber bullets in Valparaíso (which was successful in first instance, and lead to their ban for a year). I created and coordinated an association of students of law to represent victims of harassment, abuse and discrimination in the university.

Many books that never make it into my citations have changed deeply how I see the world. Among others: Eichmann In Jerusalem, Truth and Juridical Forms, An Invitation to Reflective Sociology, King Kong Theory, Disgrace, and Waiting for the Barbarians. Songs from Violeta Parra, Victor Jara, Silvio Rodríguez, Portavoz, Anita Tijoux, among others give me purpose when I forget I have one.








I sometimes paint or do clay in my free time. Some of the products are here.

title: Natalia Cabrera-Morales
text:
I am a Chilean PhD candidate in Criminology at the University of Cambridge, a lawyer, and hold a master’s in Sociology and an Mphil in Criminological Research.
I graduated from law in the top 10% of the cohort. I was the second best student of the MPhil in Criminological Research (2023-2024), being appointed as Manuel López-Rey proxime accesserunt, and an Emily Davis Scholar at Girton College. I studied my MPhil with a full scholarship from ANID-Cambridge trust and I am fully funded by Cambridge Trust for my PhD.
My PhD research focuses on police misconduct but I am generally interested in criminal justice institutions, under the supervision of Dr. Charles Lanfear.
Further projects involve gender and crime against women.
I am passionate about methodology, having experience in practice and teaching with both quantitative and qualitative methods. I am a mostly self-taught programmer, working in R and Python. However, one of my favourite academic activities is teaching.

title: Natalia Cabrera-Morales
text: I am a Chilean PhD candidate in Criminology at the University of Cambridge, a lawyer, and hold a master’s in Sociology and an Mphil in Criminological Research.

title: PhD
text:
I am generally interested in macro-micro relationships in explaining crime systemically. I am curious about the misconduct of criminal justice institutions but also about exploring under what conditions they can promote (not control) crimes by citizens.
Currently, my research focuses on police misconduct. I challenge individual and cultural explanations and propose that the institution itself can promote the misconduct of its members. In this line, I am developing a set of tests to identify the effect of the institution distinctively from individual and group factors. Misconduct is theorised as tool of government, and therefore presumably difficult to change without addressing top-down pressures. This work extends my MPhil dissertation (approved with a distinction) to the context of Germany, US and UK (in addition to Chile).
My project develops a set of falsifiable hypotheses, that I test using quantitative methods. However, qualitative methods are integrated to build categories, provide context, and sometimes, make arguments. I also use different programming tools to aid my research, especially, web scraping and natural language processing (NER and LLMs).

title: PhD
text:

title: Research and other work
text:
My work as a researcher started right after finishing my law degree, and my first publication came even earlier, as a product of my undergraduate dissertation.
Most of my published work is theoretical and qualitative. Jointly, for these positions I carried out more than 50 interviews with criminal justice professionals, including judges of the Supreme Court of Chile. I further conducted observations of court hearings and ethnography. Additionally I designed several successful grant proposals. During my time at JSCA I drafted a proposal for IDRC that was granted half a million dollars. The project investigated conflict resolution using mixed methods.
I hope my quantitative pieces of work will be published soon, but there is a library I created to structure unestructured data available on my Github.
In winter 2025-2026 I received a scholarship for an academic visit at the Max Planck Institute for Crime, Security and Law. In summer 2025 I was selected to participate in the Summer School of Computational Social Sciences in Lake Como.
